stem: The sequence \( a_n = \dfrac{[[p]]n + [[q]]}{[[r]]n + [[s]]} \) can be shown to be Cauchy. Since \( \mathbb{R} \) is complete, every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges. Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n \).
solution: Divide numerator and denominator by \( n \): \( a_n = \dfrac{[[p]] + [[q]]/n}{[[r]] + [[s]]/n} \to \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[r]]} = [[answer]] \) as \( n\to\infty \), which is the limit guaranteed to exist by completeness.
distractor_rationales: This uses the constant terms \( q/s \) instead of the leading-coefficient ratio.
This mixes the leading numerator coefficient with the constant denominator term.
This inverts the correct ratio of leading coefficients.
This assumes the limit is 0, which is only true when the numerator's degree is lower than the denominator's, not here.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Define a sequence by \( x_1 = [[x0]] \) and \( x_{n+1} = \dfrac{x_n + [[k]]}{[[m]]} \) for \( n \ge 1 \). Because consecutive terms satisfy \( |x_{n+1}-x_n| \le \dfrac{1}{[[m]]}|x_n - x_{n-1}| \) with \( \dfrac{1}{[[m]]} < 1 \), the sequence is Cauchy, so by completeness of \( \mathbb{R} \) it converges to the fixed point \( L \) of \( f(x) = \dfrac{x+[[k]]}{[[m]]} \). Find \( L \).
solution: A fixed point satisfies \( L = \dfrac{L+[[k]]}{[[m]]} \), so \( [[m]]L = L + [[k]] \), giving \( L([[m]]-1) = [[k]] \) and \( L = \dfrac{[[k]]}{[[m]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides \( k \) by \( m \) directly instead of by \( m-1 \).
This uses \( m+1 \) in the denominator instead of \( m-1 \).
This has the wrong sign on the correct denominator.
This is off by one from the correct fixed point.
This is off by one from the correct fixed point.

stem: The sequence \( a_n = \dfrac{[[c]]}{n} \) is Cauchy in \( \mathbb{R} \). Using the standard estimate \( |a_n - a_m| \le \dfrac{[[c]]}{N} \) for all \( n, m \ge N \), find the smallest positive integer \( N \) for which this estimate guarantees \( |a_n - a_m| < \dfrac{1}{[[k]]} \).
solution: We need \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{N} < \dfrac{1}{[[k]]} \), i.e. \( N > [[c]]\cdot[[k]] \). The smallest integer satisfying this is \( N = [[c]]\cdot[[k]] + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( N = ck \), which only gives \( c/N = 1/k \), not strictly less.
This adds the two quantities instead of multiplying and adding one.
This is one less than the correct threshold.
This overshoots the correct threshold by \( c \).
This undershoots the correct threshold by \( k \).
